metadata.file_name: 51. Jewsbury et al. (2025). Irt‑latent regression with many predictors: limits and solutions.pdf
metadata.title: Irt‑latent regression with many predictors: limits and solutions

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: This paper is a simulation and theoretical study of IRT-latent regression and plausible values (PVs) in large-scale assessment settings. It generates synthetic item responses and contextual predictors for N=1000 simulated test takers per simulated assessment across multiple design conditions (e.g., varying number of items J, number of predictors P, and missingness mechanisms). Because the analysis is simulation-based and does not use any real ILSA microdata sampling frame, there is no application of complex survey weights or replicate-weight procedures (e.g., BRR/jackknife) to produce population-representative estimates. The study therefore does not report or require weight variables; any “weights” discussed are conceptual (e.g., PV generation and regression parameter estimation), not survey design weights.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Simulation study: for each of 1000 simulated assessments within each condition, the authors generated data for N=1000 test takers. Each test taker received item responses to a subset of a 40-item pool, with missingness introduced under either a No missing condition (all test takers have J observed responses) or a Missing condition (70% have J observed responses and 10% each have 1, 2, or 3 observed responses), assuming Missing Completely at Random (MCAR). No real-country sampling frame or analytic subsample filtering from an ILSA dataset is described.

main_findings.dataset_used: Simulated IRT-latent regression (N=1000; 40 items; PVs via mirt fscores)
main_findings.target_variable: Population variance and extreme percentiles of latent proficiency (PV-based estimators)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Not reported as a single aggregate metric; examples include mean population variance overestimation of 52.7% in the J=4 difficult 3PL with Missing condition and ~2.4% in the J=4 moderate 2PL with No missing condition. Additional analysis (Population mean of latent proficiency (PV-based estimator)): Not reported (simulation bias patterns reported; e.g., variance overestimation up to 52.7% in worst conditions). Additional analysis (Group mean proficiency for focal groups (PV-based estimator)): Not reported as a single metric; reported qualitative bias direction (e.g., lowest group G1 biased downward more than highest group G5 biased upward) and stronger bias growth for low J and Missing conditions.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using simulated IRT-latent regression data with PV generation, the study leveraged number of predictors (P), number of items (J), missingness condition (MCAR), and IRT item model type to estimate latent proficiency variance and extreme percentiles from PVs, finding that variances and extreme percentiles (e.g., 10th/90th) are far more sensitive to increasing P than means and moderate percentiles, with the largest biases occurring when a substantial minority of test takers have low measurement precision (difficult 3PL and Missing conditions).

main_findings.dataset_used: Simulated IRT-latent regression (PV-based vs plug-in estimators)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Not reported as a single scalar; the paper reports that PV-based variance estimators are not consistent with plug-in variance estimators and are more positively biased, with the difference positively related to approximated posterior variances of regression parameters used in PV generation.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using simulated IRT-latent regression the study compared PV-based and plug-in estimators for variance and found that PV-based variance estimators produce additional positive bias relative to plug-in estimators, driven by regression-parameter measurement error (especially when P is large), indicating that PV generation conditional on sampled regression parameters can inflate variance estimates.

data.outcome_summary: This paper is a simulation/theory study of IRT-latent regression with many predictors and its impact on PV-based inferences in large-scale assessments. Across conditions, increasing the number of predictors (P) relative to the effective measurement information (small J and/or the Missing condition) produces estimation instability: mean biases become more negative in difficult 3PL and Missing settings, while variance and extreme percentiles are much more sensitive than means and moderate percentiles. The largest example reported is substantial overestimation of population variance (up to 52.7%) in the J=4 difficult 3PL with Missing condition, contrasted with near-robustness in the J=4 moderate 2PL with No missing condition (~2.4% overestimation). The study also shows that PV-based variance estimators are not consistent with plug-in variance estimators when PVs are generated using sampled regression parameters, adding positive bias tied to regression-parameter posterior uncertainty. No ML predictive modeling is trained; the focus is methodological limits of the IRT-latent regression/PV framework.
